---
title: "Concepts"
---

```{r}
#| include: false
library(tabviz)
```

The mental models that make tabviz easier to use. Read these once and the rest of the API will feel obvious.

## Pages

| Page | What you'll learn |
|------|-------------------|
| [Data Flow](data-flow.qmd) | How a `data.frame` becomes JSON, then a Svelte widget — and what that means for column references, formulas, and grouping. |
| [Styling Cascade](styling-cascade.qmd) | The four-layer precedence model: theme defaults → row → cell → marker. Where to put a style depends on which layer you want to override. |

## At a glance

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    A["data.frame"] --> B["tabviz()"]
    B --> C["WebSpec (S7)"]
    C --> D["JSON"]
    D --> E["Svelte widget"]

    style B fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style C fill:#0891b2,color:#fff
    style E fill:#ff3e00,color:#fff
```

`tabviz()` is the only entry point you need. Everything else — `col_*()`, `viz_*()`, `web_theme_*()`, `set_*()` — builds the spec that `tabviz()` consumes.

## Two ways to configure a plot

You can pass everything to `tabviz()` directly:

```r
tabviz(data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
                            scale = "log", null_value = 1)),
  row_bold = ~ pval < 0.05,
  marker_color = ~ ifelse(upper < 1, "#16a34a", "#64748b"),
  theme = web_theme_jama()
)
```

Or build it up with fluent modifiers:

```r
tabviz(data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
                            scale = "log", null_value = 1))
) |>
  set_row_style(bold = ~ pval < 0.05) |>
  set_marker_style(color = ~ ifelse(upper < 1, "#16a34a", "#64748b")) |>
  set_theme("jama")
```

Both produce the same widget. Pick whichever reads better at the call site.
